data.title: Binance says US midterms could boost Bitcoin and stocks
data.description:
The US midterm elections may be the next catalyst to kickstart the crypto and stock market recovery, according to historical data shared by Binance Research.

According to a Wednesday report from Binance Research, US midterm election cycles have historically been followed by strong rebounds in stocks and Bitcoin (BTC), potentially setting up a recovery window for risk assets after the 2026 vote.

The 12 months following US midterm elections have resulted in an average 19% rise in the S&P500 and 54% rise for Bitcoin in the three post-midterm years on record.

Binance Research said the year following the US midterms may prove the “strongest window in the cycle,” arguing that markets have historically rallied after election outcomes remove a major source of political uncertainty.

“Once election outcomes are determined and uncertainty is resolved, markets have historically staged powerful rallies.”

Bitcoin logged negative returns during previous midterm years, including a 56% drawdown in 2014, 73% decline in 2018 and a 64% retracement in 2022, but historic patterns showed a rebound in the following years.

The report comes nearly eight months before the Nov. 3 US midterm elections, which will determine the makeup of the 120th Congress.

Binance said near-term market direction is more likely to be driven by the conflict involving the US, Israel and Iran, warning that further escalation could push oil prices higher and keep risk assets under pressure.

Oil spike adds to market stress

Crude oil price briefly surged to $95 per barrel on Thursday as the conflict entered its 13th day, according to data from Trading Economics.

The price surge followed reports of Iran stepping up its attacks against energy infrastructure, as two fuel tankers were scorched by explosive-laden Iranian boats, Reuters reported earlier on Thursday.

A spokesperson for Iran’s military command told the news outlet that the world should prepare for oil prices of $200 per barrel due to the instability caused by the US.

The jump came a day after the International Energy Agency said member countries would carry out a 400 million-barrel emergency stock release, the largest coordinated drawdown on record.

Global markets in “wait-and-see” phase amid geopolitical escalations

The ongoing developments in the Middle East remain the key driver for global risk sentiment, as uncertainty surrounding energy supply and military escalations left markets in a “wait-and-see phase where policy and geopolitical risks intersect,” analysts at crypto derivatives exchange Bitunix told Cointelegraph:

“Currently, BTC is fluctuating repeatedly below the $70,000 level, indicating that market activity remains dominated by liquidity sweeps both above and below.”

The market structure suggests that Bitcoin will remain bound to this range until “macro events provide clearer directional signals,” the analysts said.

data.title: ZAWYA-PRESSR: Ajman Chamber commends growth of iron and metal industries sector and supports its expansion into external markets
data.description:
Mohamed Ali Al Janahi, Acting Director General of the Ajman Chamber, praised the rapid growth and remarkable expansion of the iron, steel, and metal industries sector, emphasizing the success of local factories in strengthening their competitive advantage and expanding the reach of their products to multiple international markets. He noted that the sector is a key pillar of industrial and economic development, contributing to developmental projects, strengthening industrial supply chains, and meeting the needs of construction, infrastructure, and other vital sectors.

His remarks were made during the launch of the first commercial export shipment to the Gulf region by BelRemaitha Steel Factory in Ajman. The event was attended by Abdullah Abdul Mohsen Al Nuaimi, Director of Government Communication at Ajman Chamber, Dr. Adel Belremeitha, Founder and Chairman of BelRemaitha Steel Factory, along with several factory officials.

During the visit, attendees toured the factory’s various departments, where they were briefed on the key products and advanced technologies used in the production lines, as well as the factory’s expansion plans and its efforts to enhance the reach of its products in regional and global markets. The factory is one of the leading specialized facilities in the iron, steel, and metal industries sector in the Emirate of Ajman and the United Arab Emirates.

Al Janahi also highlighted the advanced manufacturing capabilities available in Ajman and the UAE within the iron, steel, and metal industries sector, noting that factories rely on the latest environmentally friendly industrial technologies and implement best practices in energy and resource efficiency. These efforts contribute to environmental sustainability, enhance the competitiveness of the industrial sector, increase productivity, and support national efforts to build an advanced industrial economy based on innovation and modern technologies.

He added: “The Ajman Chamber is keen to directly support the industrial sector, particularly in light of the rapid growth it is witnessing. The number of iron, steel, and metal industries factories in Ajman reached 190 factories in 2025, while membership of newly registered factories grew by 28%, reflecting the sector’s remarkable expansion. The total number of Ajman Chamber memberships held by factories and companies associated with the iron, steel, and metal industries reached 745 memberships in 2025, marking an annual growth rate of approximately 5%.”

He further affirmed that the Chamber works to enhance the participation of factories and companies in specialized international exhibitions and events, which helps promote their products, open new export opportunities, and build commercial partnerships. In addition, the Chamber provides platforms and forums that facilitate communication with international investors and partners, thereby strengthening the competitiveness of local products in regional and global markets.

Dr. Belremeitha stated that the factory’s products include "reinforcement bars, alloys, and pipes", explaining that the factory’s strategy focuses on several key pillars, including "enhancing local production capabilities, building strategic partnerships, and developing operational processes supported by advanced technologies". The factory also aims to establish regional distribution centers, which will help expand market access and further enhance the competitiveness of its products.

Send us your press releases to pressrelease.zawya@lseg.com

data.title: Ultragenyx's gene therapy helps control ammonia levels in late-stage study
data.description:
Ultragenyx Pharmaceutical said on Thursday its experimental gene therapy helped patients in a late-stage trial to better control ammonia levels linked to a rare inherited disorder.

The therapy, called DTX301, improved a key measure of ammonia control by 18% after 36 weeks in patients with ornithine transcarbamylase, or OTC deficiency, compared with a placebo, the company said.

OTC deficiency is a rare inherited disorder caused by the lack of a liver enzyme that helps clear ammonia, leading to a toxic buildup that can trigger confusion, seizures and coma.

The company said the drug was generally well tolerated. The most common side effects were liver-related problems that were managed with steroids.

data.title: U.S. Housing Starts Jumped in January
data.description:
(12:31 GMT) *US Jan Housing Starts +7.2% To 1.487M; Consensus -3.8%
(12:31 GMT) *Building Permits -5.4% To 1.376M Rate In Jan
(12:41 GMT) U.S. Housing Starts Jumped in January

By Don Nico Forbes

Housing starts jumped in January, as headwinds in the homebuilding sector continued to fade at the start of the year. Here are the main takeaways from the Commerce Department's report published Thursday:

  • Housing starts, a gauge of new residential construction, rose 7.2% in the month to 1.49 million. A consensus of economists polled by The Wall Street Journal expected a total of 1.35 million starts.
  • Starts rose 9.5% on year.
  • Residential permits, another measure of trends in housing construction, fell 5.4% to 1.38 million. A consensus of economists forecast 1.41 million permits.

Write to Don Nico Forbes at don.forbes@wsj.com

data.title: Global carmakers book $70 billion hit from EV rollbacks
data.description:
By Alessandro Parodi

Global carmakers have booked over $70 billion in writedowns in the past year as they scale back electric vehicle ambitions on a tough U.S. market under President Donald Trump, price wars in China and a more complex mix of vehicle types in Europe.

The latest to join the growing pile is Japan's Honda , which on Thursday said it expected a hit of $15.7 billion over the next few years to restructure its EV business.

CEO Toshihiro Mibe said the group would focus on hybrids, echoing moves by peers Stellantis , Ford , General Motors and Volkswagen , which backtracked on their EV ambitions or launched new combustion engine models.

Legacy carmakers are struggling to keep up with new entrants, especially from China, and watered down electrification targets in Europe and in particular the U.S., a key market where the EV shift has stalled sharply.

HONDA

Japan's second-largest automaker said on March 12 it expects to lose 2.5 trillion yen ($15.7 billion) over the next few years as it scraps the development of some planned EV models.

It added that it expects to lose as much as 570 billion yen in the year to the end of March, versus a previous forecast for a profit of 550 billion yen.

STELLANTIS

The Franco-Italian automaker booked on February 6 its huge writedown, the biggest yet, which it said was linked to rejigging its product lineup to meet consumer demand and new emission regulations in the United States.

The writedown includes payments of approximately 6.5 billion euros expected to be made over the next four years.

FORD MOTOR

The Dearborn, Michigan-based company said in December it would take a $19.5 billion writedown and kill several EV models, and pivot hard into gas and hybrid models instead.

GENERAL MOTORS

The largest U.S. automaker by sales said in January it would take a $6 billion charge to unwind some electric-vehicle investments, including a $4.2 billion cash charge related to contract cancellations and settlements with suppliers.

VOLKSWAGEN/PORSCHE

Volkswagen , Europe's top carmaker, said last September it would take a 5.1 billion euro ($6 billion) hit from a far-reaching product overhaul at its Porsche unit, which delayed some EV models in favour of hybrids and combustion engine cars.

That included an around $3.5 billion impairment charge.

($1 = 0.8477 euros)

($1 = 158.6900 yen)

data.title: Tax Refunds Are Bigger This Year. How to Invest It Wisely. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Elizabeth O'Brien

Tax refunds are up this year, and so is uncertainty, raising the question: Where do you invest now?

It's an important consideration for retirees who may be grappling with conflicting market signals. Stocks and bonds have been rattled by the Iran war and the most severe energy crisis in years. Before the war, concerns about the impact of artificial intelligence and fissures in private credit had kept the S&P 500 index largely flat for the year.

Many seniors, meanwhile, are sitting on a pile of cash from tax refunds. The average refund is up more than 10% from last year to $3,742, according to the Internal Revenue Service. That number may be even higher for seniors who qualify for the new, $6,000 deduction for taxpayers 65-plus.

More than 9.2 million out of the nearly 63.5 million returns filed so far have claimed the enhanced senior deduction, the U.S. Treasury Department said on Tuesday. This extra deduction was enacted through 2028 in lieu of eliminating taxes on Social Security; eligibility begins to phase out at incomes of over $75,000 for single filers and $150,000 for joint filers.

Markets aren't likely to start recovering until the fighting winds down and oil resumes flowing from the Middle East. If the war continues, disruptions in the global oil market could push prices to levels that crimp economic growth or even tip the country into a recession, analysts say. The specter of slowing growth plus higher prices has revived fears of stagflation, a challenging environment for stocks last seen in the 1970s.

"This is a perfect 'take stock' moment," says Chris Kampitsis, a Certified Financial Planner at Barnum Financial Group's SKG Team in Elmsford, N.Y.

Before investing, check your cash cushion. Most advisors recommend that retirees keep up to two years' worth of portfolio withdrawals in cash — the amount you need to withdraw from your retirement accounts to meet your living expenses, after accounting for Social Security and other income sources. That way, your essentials are covered regardless of how the market performs, and you won't stress your portfolio by withdrawing from a declining balance.

This is distinct from your emergency fund, a separate pot to tap if you need to replace your roof or get pricey dental work like a new crown. This money should be parked in cash, whether that's a high-yield savings account, money-market account, or short-term bond fund. The right amount will vary based on your lifestyle, but having at least several thousand dollars set aside will help you from taking on credit-card debt after a budget-busting expense.

With basics set, you can invest your refund.

It's hard to make sector bets, given all of the crosscurrents in the economy, says Andrew Crowell, vice chairman of wealth management at D.A. Davidson in Pasadena, Calif. It might be tempting to load up in defensive areas like healthcare, consumer staples, and utilities. But timing is tricky. While those sectors historically lose less than the broad market in downturns of at least 20%, they underperform coming out of bear markets, according to research from Sam Stovall, chief investment strategist at CFRA Research.

Crowell likes balanced funds that replicate the classic 60% stock/40% bond portfolio. Low-cost mutual funds include the Vanguard LifeStrategy Moderate Growth, which invests in 60% stock and 40% bonds, with each bucket containing a mixture of domestic and international securities.

Bonds aren't acting like a haven now. Yields have rallied, pushing down prices (which move inversely). Investors worry about the inflationary impact of higher oil prices, complicating potential interest-rate cuts by the Federal Reserve. While the market still expects a couple of quarter-point cuts this year, the odds have fallen since the war started.

Bonds can still reduce your portfolio's volatility, however, and most advisors recommend some exposure. Short-term funds pose the least interest-rate risk. The iShares Core 1-5 Year USD Bond exchange-traded fund, for instance, yields 3.9% and is up 0.6% this year on a total-return basis.

If you want stock market exposure with a bit of protection, consider "buffered" ETFs. These funds track indexes like the S&P 500 but use options to limit investors' downside. Expense ratios are higher than simple ETFs, however, and the funds put caps on gains over a defined period.

Clark Bellin, president of Bellwether Wealth in Lincoln, Neb., uses ETFs like those from Innovator ETFs, such as the Innovator U.S. Equity Power Buffer — March. Each month, the company issues a new edition; to get the full protection, it's important to invest at the beginning of the month.

The ETFs can help you stay invested through a market rough patch (if you would otherwise panic and sell). But they shouldn't represent more than 30% of your equity allocation, Bellin says.

Write to Elizabeth O'Brien at elizabeth.obrien@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Fund firm M&G warns about 'overcrowded' US private credit market
data.description:
Key points:
  • M&G says very limited private credit exposure to software companies
  • CEO highlights differences between US and European markets
  • Company reports flat operating profit, improved fund inflows

By Iain Withers

British insurer and money manager M&G said on Thursday it had less than 2% exposure to software companies in its private assets arm and contrasted Europe's private credit funds with an "overcrowded" U.S. market, after posting full-year results.

Private credit has expanded rapidly into a $2 trillion industry, but growing concerns about transparency and credit quality have led more investors to pull out of some U.S. funds.

Concerns about software companies backed heavily by credit lenders and seen as vulnerable to artificial intelligence startups have added to the trend.

"We need to distinguish the U.S. from Europe. We're talking about two very different markets," M&G CEO Andrea Rossi told Reuters. "The problems you have seen in the U.S., it's mainly been driven by software exposure disrupted by AI."

The U.S. private credit market is also more mature, competitive and overcrowded, Rossi said, adding that Europe's market was still developing and remains dominated by bank lending.

M&G's Europe-focused 27 billion pounds ($36 billion) of credit assets had strong underwriting, he added.

Some of the company's other private assets have nonetheless faced issues, including a potential hit on funds exposed to residential ground rents that face a proposed UK government cap.

CLIENT INFLOWS PICK UP

M&G reported flat operating profit of 838 million pounds for 2025 and reiterated its 2027 financial targets.

The company said it was starting to benefit from a tie-up with Japanese insurer Dai-ichi Life struck last year, helping drive up net inflows from open business to 7.8 billion pounds, compared with 1.9 billion pounds of outflows previously.

RBC analysts said the flows showed strong momentum, with earnings partly offset by lower performance fees and higher costs in asset management.

Shares were last down 2.9%, versus a 0.5% fall in the FTSE 100 index.

Deutsche Bank separately said on Thursday its private credit portfolio had grown to 26 billion euros ($30 billion) and highlighted risks to the sector.

($1 = 0.7468 pounds)

($1 = 0.8668 euros)

data.title: reAlpha Tech 2025 10-K: Revenue $4.52M, EPS $(0.23)
data.description:
reAlpha Tech reported fiscal 2025 revenue of $4.52 million and a net loss per share of $(0.23), reflecting a sharp revenue increase driven by mortgage brokerage, AiChat subscriptions and the Prevu acquisition while earnings were weighed down by elevated operating expenses and a significant discontinued operations loss. Revenue rose ~376% year‑over‑year, but operating loss widened as spending on salaries, marketing and professional fees increased. The company also recorded an $18.34 million loss from discontinued operations that materially affected net results.

Financial Highlights

  • Revenue: $4.518 million, a 376% increase vs. 2024 driven by mortgage brokerage, AiChat subscriptions and Prevu contribution.
  • Gross profit: $2.451 million; gross margin 54% (down from 68% in 2024).
  • Operating loss: $(16.007) million; operating expenses increased ~145% due to higher salaries, marketing and professional fees.
  • Net loss: $(17.590) million, which includes an $18.340 million loss from discontinued operations.
  • Net loss per share — basic: $(0.23); diluted: $(0.23) (weighted-average shares 76.316 million).

Business Highlights

  • Revenue growth and sources: Revenues jumped ~376% YoY to $4.52 million in 2025, driven primarily by mortgage brokerage activity, subscription revenue from AiChat, and contributions from the Prevu acquisition.
  • Strategic shift: Transitioned from an asset‑heavy short‑term rental model to an AI‑powered, vertically integrated homebuying platform encompassing realty, mortgage and title services.
  • Platform and AI development: Launched proprietary AI concierge “Claire” and internal loan officer/engagement tools aimed at automating origination and customer workflows.
  • Acquisitions and geographic expansion: Integrated acquisitions including reAlpha Nepal, AiChat, reAlpha Mortgage, Prevu and Hyperfast; now offering services across 35 states plus D.C. with plans for nationwide rollout.
  • Operational milestones: Recognized short‑term rental operations as discontinued, completed the Prevu acquisition, and signed a proposed InstaMortgage merger to add lending capabilities.

Original SEC Filing: reAlpha Tech Corp. [ AIRE ] - 10-K - Mar. 12, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.
